comments.text: If we consider\[u_r(n)=\#\Big\{k:\#\{p\leq n:\nu_p(n!)=k\}=r\Big\},\]which is the number of exponents in the factorization of $n!$ such that each exponent $k$ has $r$ representatives, then\[h(n) = \sum_{1\leq r}u_r(n)\]and we have\[u_r(n)\sim c_r\sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}, \qquad c_r=\sqrt{2\pi}\left(-1\right)^{r+1}\binom{2-\sqrt2}{r},\]by the same Cramer model as Tao (the other comment). We also get that\[\pi(n) =\sum_{1\leq r}r\,u_r(n),\]which is interesting. Finally, a computation up to $10^8$ shows that $h(n+1)-h(n)\in \{-1,0,1\}$, which seems true always, though a proof seems intractable to me.

One can predict the value of $c$ using the Cramer random model for the primes. Firstly some reductions. Legendre's formula states that $$k_i = \lfloor n/p_i \rfloor + \lfloor n/p_i^2 \rfloor + \dots.$$ Both the main term $\lfloor n/p_i \rfloor$ and the tail $\lfloor n/p_i^2 \rfloor + \dots$ are non-increasing in $i$; but the latter only changes value $O(n^{1/3})$ times (consider the cases $p_i \leq n^{1/3}$ and $p_i \geq n^{1/3}$ separately). Thus, deleting this term only modifies $h$ by a lower order error, and the problem is equivalent to that of counting the number of distinct values of $\lfloor n/p_i \rfloor$. For any $\varepsilon$, the primes less than $\varepsilon \sqrt{n \log n}$ are only $O(\varepsilon (n/\log n)^{1/2})$ in number, and for the primes $p_i$ larger than $\varepsilon^{-1} \sqrt{n \log n}$ the value of $\lfloor n/p_i\rfloor$ is $O(\varepsilon (n/\log n)^{1/2})$. So up to lower order error we can restrict attention to the primes $p_i \asymp \sqrt{n \log n}$, for which $k_i \asymp (n/\log n)^{1/2}$. A given $k \asymp (n/\log n)^{1/2}$ will be of the form $\lfloor n/p_i \rfloor$ iff there is a prime in the interval $(n/(k+1),n/k]$. If $k = t (n/\log n)^{1/2}$, this is an interval of length approximately $t^{-2} \log n$ of numbers of size $\asymp \sqrt{n \log n}$ - so the prime density here is approximately $2/\log n$. The Cramer random model then predicts a prime to be present with probability approximately $1-e^{-2t^{-2}}$. So this leads to the prediction $$ c = \int_0^\infty 1 - e^{-2t^{-2}}\ dt = \sqrt{2\pi} = 2.50663\dots$$ (the value here was provided by an LLM and verified by Wolfram alpha; it can be derived from the usual gaussian integral identities by a routine substitution and the Feynman trick of replacing $2$ by a parameter that one then differentiates in.) A quick AI request to plot the ratio between $h(n)$ and $\sqrt{2\pi} (n/\log n)^{1/2}$ seems to back up this prediction. Proving this claim unconditionally, even on average in $n$, would likely require information about the distribution of prime gaps that currently is only available under strong hypotheses such as the prime tuples conjecture. EDIT: By plugging in the AI-provided values of $h$ into the OEIS, I discovered that the function $h(n)$ is OEIS A071626. As observed in the paper linked there, the deletion of the tail in fact does not affect $h$ at all (basically because the tail only jumps when the main term does). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
